title: JNodeProperties

tex_statement.html: With the notations def_3_2 the restrictions in definition def_3_1 mean that the nodes $j \in J$ will not have any arrowheads pointing towards them: $ j \hus v \notin G$. Nodes $j \in J$ can only point towards nodes $v \in V$: edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed. Furthermore, no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent.

tex_proof.raw:
% Claim proof file for row claim_3_1 -- "JNodeProperties".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solving_current_row.py at row start. A
% manager agent dedicated to writing the TeX proof fills in the body below.
% The proof must:
%   - Stay close to the lecture notes' proof if one exists (always search
%     the LN first for a `\begin{proof}` block immediately following the
%     claim; copy / lightly edit when present).
%   - Otherwise use the LN paradigm and cite prior claims by their `ref`.
%   - Be self-contained enough that a mathematician can follow it without
%     re-reading the LN.
%   - Have no `sorry`, `omitted`, or "obvious" shortcuts.
%
% Compiles standalone via the `subfiles` package.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_1 (proof, with statement restated for readability)
% title: JNodeProperties
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_1}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_1}

% --- statement (restated) ---------------------------------------------------
\begin{Rem}[JNodeProperties]
With the notations \refrow{def_3_2} the restrictions in definition \refrow{def_3_1} mean that the nodes $j \in J$ will not have any arrowheads pointing towards them: $ j \hus v \notin G$. Nodes $j \in J$ can only point towards nodes $v \in V$: edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed. Furthermore, no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent.
\end{Rem}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
% The LN states this as a remark immediately after \refrow{def_3_2}
% (graphs.tex line 84-89) and provides no proof block; the statement is
% the unfolding-only consequence of the structural fields of
% \refrow{def_3_1}. The proof below is constructed from scratch in the
% LN paradigm: every step is one notation-unfolding of \refrow{def_3_2}
% followed by one of the inclusions $E \ins (J \cup V) \x V$,
% $L \ins V \x V$, or the disjointness $J \cap V = \emptyset$ from
% \refrow{def_3_1}.

\begin{proof}
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG as in \refrow{def_3_1}. We verify the
three assertions in turn.

\medskip
\noindent\emph{(1) No arrowhead points at any $j \in J$:
$j \hus v \notin G$ for all $v \in G$.}
Suppose for contradiction that $j \in J$ and $j \hus v \in G$ for some
$v$. By \refrow{def_3_2}, $j \hus v \in G$ means that either
$j \hut v \in G$ or $j \huh v \in G$.
\begin{itemize}
  \item If $j \hut v \in G$, then by \refrow{def_3_2} $(v, j) \in E$.
        By \refrow{def_3_1} we have $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$,
        so the second coordinate of any pair in $E$ lies in $V$; hence
        $j \in V$.
  \item If $j \huh v \in G$, then by \refrow{def_3_2} $(j, v) \in L$.
        By \refrow{def_3_1} we have $L \ins V \times V$, so both
        coordinates of any pair in $L$ lie in $V$; hence $j \in V$.
\end{itemize}
Either way $j \in J \cap V$, contradicting the disjointness of $J$ and
$V$ stipulated in \refrow{def_3_1}.

\medskip
\noindent\emph{(2) Directed edges out of an input node target $V$:
if $j \in J$ and $j \tuh w \in G$, then $w \in V$.}
By \refrow{def_3_2}, $j \tuh w \in G$ means $(j, w) \in E$. Since
$E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$ by \refrow{def_3_1}, the second coordinate
$w$ lies in $V$.

\medskip
\noindent\emph{(3) No two input nodes are adjacent: for all
$j_1, j_2 \in J$, $j_1 \sus j_2 \notin G$.}
Suppose for contradiction that $j_1, j_2 \in J$ and
$j_1 \sus j_2 \in G$. By \refrow{def_3_2}, $j_1 \sus j_2 \in G$ means
one of the three sub-cases $j_1 \tuh j_2 \in G$,
$j_1 \hut j_2 \in G$, or $j_1 \huh j_2 \in G$.
\begin{itemize}
  \item If $j_1 \tuh j_2 \in G$, then $(j_1, j_2) \in E$, which by
        \refrow{def_3_2} is exactly $j_2 \hut j_1 \in G$. In particular
        $j_2 \hus j_1 \in G$, contradicting part (1) applied to
        $j_2 \in J$.
  \item If $j_1 \hut j_2 \in G$, then $j_1 \hus j_2 \in G$ directly
        by the definition of $\hus$ in \refrow{def_3_2}, contradicting
        part (1) applied to $j_1 \in J$.
  \item If $j_1 \huh j_2 \in G$, then $j_1 \hus j_2 \in G$ (again
        directly from \refrow{def_3_2}), contradicting part (1)
        applied to $j_1 \in J$.
\end{itemize}
In each case we reach a contradiction, so $j_1 \sus j_2 \notin G$; by
the adjacency definition immediately following \refrow{def_3_2}, this
is precisely the statement that no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent.
\end{proof}

\end{document}
tex_proof.html:
Remark — JNodeProperties

With the notations def_3_2 the restrictions in definition def_3_1 mean that the nodes $j \in J$ will not have any arrowheads pointing towards them: $ j \hus v \notin G$. Nodes $j \in J$ can only point towards nodes $v \in V$: edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed. Furthermore, no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent.

Proof.

Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG as in def_3_1. We verify the three assertions in turn.

(1) No arrowhead points at any $j \in J$: $j \hus v \notin G$ for all $v \in G$. Suppose for contradiction that $j \in J$ and $j \hus v \in G$ for some $v$. By def_3_2, $j \hus v \in G$ means that either $j \hut v \in G$ or $j \huh v \in G$.

  • If $j \hut v \in G$, then by def_3_2 $(v, j) \in E$. By def_3_1 we have $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$, so the second coordinate of any pair in $E$ lies in $V$; hence $j \in V$.
  • If $j \huh v \in G$, then by def_3_2 $(j, v) \in L$. By def_3_1 we have $L \ins V \times V$, so both coordinates of any pair in $L$ lie in $V$; hence $j \in V$.

Either way $j \in J \cap V$, contradicting the disjointness of $J$ and $V$ stipulated in def_3_1.

(2) Directed edges out of an input node target $V$: if $j \in J$ and $j \tuh w \in G$, then $w \in V$. By def_3_2, $j \tuh w \in G$ means $(j, w) \in E$. Since $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$ by def_3_1, the second coordinate $w$ lies in $V$.

(3) No two input nodes are adjacent: for all $j_1, j_2 \in J$, $j_1 \sus j_2 \notin G$. Suppose for contradiction that $j_1, j_2 \in J$ and $j_1 \sus j_2 \in G$. By def_3_2, $j_1 \sus j_2 \in G$ means one of the three sub-cases $j_1 \tuh j_2 \in G$, $j_1 \hut j_2 \in G$, or $j_1 \huh j_2 \in G$.

  • If $j_1 \tuh j_2 \in G$, then $(j_1, j_2) \in E$, which by def_3_2 is exactly $j_2 \hut j_1 \in G$. In particular $j_2 \hus j_1 \in G$, contradicting part (1) applied to $j_2 \in J$.
  • If $j_1 \hut j_2 \in G$, then $j_1 \hus j_2 \in G$ directly by the definition of $\hus$ in def_3_2, contradicting part (1) applied to $j_1 \in J$.
  • If $j_1 \huh j_2 \in G$, then $j_1 \hus j_2 \in G$ (again directly from def_3_2), contradicting part (1) applied to $j_1 \in J$.

In each case we reach a contradiction, so $j_1 \sus j_2 \notin G$; by the adjacency definition immediately following def_3_2, this is precisely the statement that no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent.

lean_code_with_comments:
theorem no_arrowhead_into_input
    {G : CDMG α} {j : α} (hj : j ∈ G.J) (v : α) :
    ¬ hus G j v := by
  intro hhus
  have hjV : j ∈ G.V := by
    rcases hhus with h_hut | h_huh
    · exact (G.E_subset h_hut).2
    · exact (G.L_subset h_huh).1
  exact Set.disjoint_left.mp G.disjoint_JV hj hjV

set_option linter.unusedVariables false in
theorem input_edge_target_mem_V
    {G : CDMG α} {j : α} (hj : j ∈ G.J) {w : α}
    (h : tuh G j w) : w ∈ G.V :=
  (G.E_subset h).2

theorem input_nodes_not_adjacent
    {G : CDMG α} {j₁ j₂ : α}
    (h₁ : j₁ ∈ G.J) (h₂ : j₂ ∈ G.J) :
    ¬ Adjacent G j₁ j₂ := by
  intro hAdj
  rcases hAdj with h_tuh | h_hut | h_huh
  · exact no_arrowhead_into_input h₂ j₁ (Or.inl h_tuh)
  · exact no_arrowhead_into_input h₁ j₂ (Or.inl h_hut)
  · exact no_arrowhead_into_input h₁ j₂ (Or.inr h_huh)
lean_code_without_comments:
theorem no_arrowhead_into_input
    {G : CDMG α} {j : α} (hj : j ∈ G.J) (v : α) :
    ¬ hus G j v := by
  intro hhus
  have hjV : j ∈ G.V := by
    rcases hhus with h_hut | h_huh
    · exact (G.E_subset h_hut).2
    · exact (G.L_subset h_huh).1
  exact Set.disjoint_left.mp G.disjoint_JV hj hjV

set_option linter.unusedVariables false in
theorem input_edge_target_mem_V
    {G : CDMG α} {j : α} (hj : j ∈ G.J) {w : α}
    (h : tuh G j w) : w ∈ G.V :=
  (G.E_subset h).2

theorem input_nodes_not_adjacent
    {G : CDMG α} {j₁ j₂ : α}
    (h₁ : j₁ ∈ G.J) (h₂ : j₂ ∈ G.J) :
    ¬ Adjacent G j₁ j₂ := by
  intro hAdj
  rcases hAdj with h_tuh | h_hut | h_huh
  · exact no_arrowhead_into_input h₂ j₁ (Or.inl h_tuh)
  · exact no_arrowhead_into_input h₁ j₂ (Or.inl h_hut)
  · exact no_arrowhead_into_input h₁ j₂ (Or.inr h_huh)
lean_explanation:
The file formalises a remark from the lecture notes that bundles three structural consequences of `def_3_1`'s constraint fields into one prose paragraph. We split that paragraph into three independently citable lemmas, each phrased in the prose-level edge notation of `def_3_2` and `def_3_3` rather than in raw `G.E` / `G.L` membership.

Throughout, `α : Type*` is the ambient vertex carrier — the LN's silent universe of nodes — and `G : CDMG α` is the graph (implicit, inferred from the membership hypothesis `hj : j ∈ G.J`). The variable `j` plays the role of an input node (the LN's *j ∈ J*); `v` and `w` are auxiliary vertices.

`no_arrowhead_into_input` says that for any input node `j` and any vertex `v`, the predicate `hus G j v` cannot hold. (`hus G j v`, defined in `def_3_2`, holds iff there is an arrowhead at the first argument: either an incoming directed edge or a bidirected edge.) The proof unfolds `hus` into its two disjuncts, uses `G.E_subset` / `G.L_subset` (the `def_3_1` inclusions *E ⊆ (J ∪ V) × V* and *L ⊆ V × V*) to force `j ∈ G.V`, and applies `Set.disjoint_left.mp` to the `disjoint_JV` field to obtain a contradiction.

`input_edge_target_mem_V` says that a directed edge `tuh G j w` out of an input node must target `w ∈ G.V`; its body is the one-line projection `(G.E_subset h).2`. The declaration is preceded by `set_option linter.unusedVariables false in` because the prose-named hypothesis `hj` is not actually consumed by the proof — `E_subset` alone is strong enough — but we keep `hj` explicit to scope the lemma to input nodes.

`input_nodes_not_adjacent` concludes `¬ Adjacent G j₁ j₂` for any two input nodes, matching the LN's literal "no two nodes in *J* are adjacent". The proof case-splits on the three disjuncts of `Adjacent` (which unfolds to `tuh ∨ hut ∨ huh`) and discharges each by appealing to part (1) with the appropriate input node and disjunct injection.

Two Mathlib idioms a first-time reader may not recognise: `×ˢ` is `Set.prod`, the Cartesian product of two sets (so `E ⊆ (J ∪ V) ×ˢ V` is exactly the inclusion in `def_3_1`); and `Set.disjoint_left.mp` converts a `Disjoint A B` hypothesis into the punchier form `a ∈ A → a ∉ B`.
design_choices:
**Three separate theorems, not one conjunction.** The LN bundles three independent sub-statements into one prose paragraph for readability, but in Lean each one is wanted as a separately citable lemma. A downstream proof using "directed edges out of `J` target `V`" almost never needs "no arrowhead at `J`" on the same line. Splitting the remark into three theorems keeps each one a clean one-liner at the use site and avoids the boilerplate of destructuring a tuple of conclusions.

**Conclusions phrased in the prose-level edge notation, not raw set membership.** Each theorem's conclusion uses the `def_3_2` / `def_3_3` notation that callers will already have in their hypotheses — `hus G j v`, `tuh G j w`, `Adjacent G j₁ j₂` — rather than `(j, v) ∈ G.E` and friends. Unfolding to raw set membership is one rewrite away, but doing it in the statement would force every caller to insert a corresponding unfolding step before applying the lemma.

**Universal `v` made explicit in `no_arrowhead_into_input`.** The LN reads, paraphrased, "for any vertex *v*, the predicate `hus G j v` does not hold": the universal is outside `hus`. Making `v` explicit aligns the call-site phrasing `no_arrowhead_into_input hj v` with that reading, and lets callers with a hypothesis `h : hus G j v` pass `h` directly without `@`-syntax or relying on unification.

**Unused `hj` retained in `input_edge_target_mem_V`.** The proof does not actually use `j ∈ G.J` — the inclusion `E ⊆ (J ∪ V) ×ˢ V` alone forces `w ∈ V`. We keep `hj` as an explicit binder anyway because dropping it would broaden the statement to "any directed edge's target lies in `V`", which is a different, more general lemma. The local `set_option linter.unusedVariables false in` is just there to silence the unused-binder warning for this specific case.

**No `j₁ ≠ j₂` side-condition on `input_nodes_not_adjacent`.** The LN's "no two nodes" naturally reads as "for any two, possibly equal, input nodes". Even at `j₁ = j₂`, `Adjacent G j₁ j₁` is false: the directed disjuncts force `j₁ ∈ V` via `E_subset`, and the bidirected disjunct is ruled out by the `L_irrefl` field of `def_3_1`. Adding `j₁ ≠ j₂` would weaken the lemma without simplifying any caller.
